greeting: Morning, Haniel. Two systems are built and waiting — today you make them live.
hype: No code left to write. Six deploy triggers stand between you and two live products. Pull them.
notice: Have Stripe live keys (both modes), Chatwoot token, and CT Pros QB credentials in hand before 9 AM — today's blocks gate on them.

tasks.title: DO droplet deploy (app/queue/scheduler/TLS/MySQL8, scheduler OFF)
tasks.notes: DO droplet, hand-enter 9 clients, reconcile, enable, webhooks last

prep.what: Provision a fresh DigitalOcean droplet and deploy the full AZ Team AI System — PHP/Laravel app, queue worker, nginx, TLS, MySQL 8 — with the scheduler kept OFF until reconciliation passes.
prep.understand: The stack has a queue worker (billing jobs: invoice minting, Stripe charges, Chatwoot SMS) and a cron scheduler (`billing:run-recurring`, `dispatch-reminders`, `reconcile-stripe`). Leave the scheduler OFF on first boot — it auto-mints invoices the moment it fires. You want the reconciliation gate (t39) to pass first. The CT Pros deploy scripts are the proven template for this exact stack.
approach: Provision a fresh DO droplet (Ubuntu 22.04, 2–4 GB RAM) and point the AZ Team AI domain DNS A record to its IP
SSH in, clone the azteamai-system repo, run `deploy/*.sh` to install PHP, nginx, MySQL 8
Run `php artisan migrate --force` then seed billing/company settings; verify the app boots at the domain over HTTPS
Start the queue worker via supervisor (`php artisan queue:work`); confirm it's active — leave the Laravel scheduler (crontab) OFF for now
resources: `deploy/*.sh` and `docker-compose.yml` in the azteamai-system repo
`docs/deployment.md` — step-by-step deploy guide (modeled on CT Pros)
DigitalOcean dashboard — droplet provisioning
`.env.example` in the repo as the complete env field checklist

tasks.title: Secrets in .env.production (Stripe both modes, Chatwoot, mail)

prep.what: Fill `.env.production` on the droplet with real Stripe live + test keys, Chatwoot channel token, and mail credentials — before any live traffic arrives.
prep.understand: You need Stripe in BOTH modes: live secret key for production billing, test-mode key for sandbox checks. Chatwoot needs a channel-level API token (not a user token) — that's what `NotificationChannel::chatwoot()` posts with. `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET` comes after t40 when you register the endpoint — don't set it here.
approach: Stripe Dashboard → Developers → API Keys → copy live publishable + secret key; also copy the test-mode secret key
Chatwoot admin → Settings → API Access Tokens → create or copy the channel-level token
Set `MAIL_MAILER`, `MAIL_HOST`, `MAIL_FROM_ADDRESS` for your production mail provider (SES, Mailgun, or SMTP)
Drop all secrets into `.env.production` on the droplet (chmod 600), then run `php artisan config:cache`
resources: Stripe Dashboard → Developers → API Keys (switch to live mode)
Chatwoot admin panel → Settings → API Access Tokens
`.env.example` in the azteamai-system repo as the complete field checklist
Mail provider console (AWS SES or SMTP credentials)

tasks.title: Reconciliation gate: imported AR + recurring state matches azteam_crm ground truth
tasks.notes: Reconciliation gate: imported AR + recurring state matches azteam_crm ground truth

prep.what: Verify the import (t38, already done) is accurate — AR balances, project versions, subscription state, and client details must match azteam_crm before the scheduler goes live.
prep.understand: t38 already imported CLOSED projects, versions, invoices, and client details from azteam_crm. This gate exists because the scheduler will compound any import error on its first run — it mints new invoices based on what it sees. Catch mismatches here, not in live billing.
approach: Pull an AR aging summary from azteam_crm — list every client with outstanding balance and amount
Open the new AZ Team AI System → Dashboard → AR cluster — compare total outstanding and per-client balances
Spot-check 3–5 clients: verify project versions, invoice line items, payment records, and subscription state match the source
If totals match → gate passed, proceed to t40; if discrepancies → fix the import script before enabling the scheduler
resources: azteam_crm admin panel — ground truth for all AR and client data
New AZ Team AI System on the DO droplet — Dashboard + Clients + AR Aging report
Import script output log from t38 (server logs or repo)
AR Aging report in both systems side by side in a split-screen browser

tasks.title: Enable scheduler; watch first run; register Stripe webhook LAST
tasks.notes: Enable scheduler; watch first run; register Stripe webhook LAST

prep.what: Enable the Laravel scheduler, watch the first billing sweep complete clean, then register the Stripe webhook endpoint — in exactly that order.
prep.understand: Order is non-negotiable: scheduler first, webhook second. The scheduler runs `billing:run-recurring`, `dispatch-reminders`, `reconcile-stripe`. Stripe fires events the instant you register a webhook. If `reconcile-stripe` isn't running first, that first batch of Stripe events lands with no consumer and could double-mint on retry.
approach: SSH to droplet → add the scheduler to root crontab: `* * * * * cd /path/to/app && php artisan schedule:run >> /dev/null 2>&1`
Tail `storage/logs/laravel.log` and watch the first `billing:run-recurring` sweep complete without exceptions
Stripe Dashboard → Developers → Webhooks → Add endpoint → paste `https://your-domain/stripe/webhook`, select `payment_intent.succeeded` and `payment_intent.payment_failed`
Send a Stripe test event → confirm it lands in the `stripe_events` table and is processed idempotently (no duplicate invoice)
resources: SSH access to the DO droplet
Stripe Dashboard → Developers → Webhooks
`storage/logs/laravel.log` on the server
`stripe_events` table in MySQL — verify idempotent event ingestion

tasks.title: Fill real CT Pros company contact info
tasks.notes: config/company.php + .env still hold HighTech placeholders. Set real CT Pros name/address/phone/email BEFORE any client-facing PDF or email goes out.

prep.what: Replace all inherited HighTech Tile placeholder data in `config/company.php` and `.env` with real CT Pros company info — commit this before the t27 deploy runs.
prep.understand: The clone carried HighTech's company config verbatim. That data appears on every estimate PDF, proposal letterhead, invoice, and outbound email. One deploy before this fix and Rafa's clients receive documents with the wrong company name, address, and contact info on every document.
approach: Open `config/company.php` in the ct-pros-sys repo — update name, address, phone, email with real CT Pros values (confirm with Rafa if anything is uncertain)
Search `.env` and `.env.example` for `MAIL_FROM_NAME`, `MAIL_FROM_ADDRESS`, and any `COMPANY_` vars still referencing HighTech
Boot the app locally → Leads → any lead → generate a sample estimate PDF → confirm CT Pros branding in the header
Commit and push to ct-pros-sys — this commit MUST land before t27 deploy
resources: CT Pros real company info (name, address, phone, email) — get from Rafa if needed
`config/company.php` in the ct-pros-sys repo
`.env.example` as the full env var reference
Estimate PDF preview: Leads → any lead → Estimates → download

tasks.title: Connect CT Pros's own QuickBooks (fresh OAuth)
tasks.notes: Inherited tokens were HighTech's and never carried over. Run a fresh OAuth connect against CT Pros's own QBO company so all syncs hit the right books.

prep.what: Connect CT Pros's own QuickBooks Online company to the app via a fresh OAuth flow, replacing the now-invalid inherited HighTech tokens.
prep.understand: The QB tokens from HighTech were never carried over and are scoped to HighTech's QBO. CT Pros has its own QBO account; the connection must point there. Set `QUICKBOOKS_ENVIRONMENT=production` so syncs hit live books. If the domain changes in t27, update the Intuit OAuth redirect URI in the Developer Console to match before running this flow.
approach: Open `.env` → confirm `QUICKBOOKS_ENVIRONMENT=production` and correct `QUICKBOOKS_CLIENT_ID` + `QUICKBOOKS_CLIENT_SECRET` (Intuit app credentials for CT Pros)
Open the app → Settings → QuickBooks → click 'Connect to QuickBooks'
Complete the Intuit OAuth flow with CT Pros's actual QBO account login (get creds from Rafa)
After connect: run `php artisan quickbooks:sync` → confirm no errors and the sync hits CT Pros's books
resources: CT Pros QBO account credentials — Rafa provides the login
App Settings → QuickBooks page
`.env` vars: `QUICKBOOKS_CLIENT_ID`, `QUICKBOOKS_CLIENT_SECRET`, `QUICKBOOKS_ENVIRONMENT`
Intuit Developer Console — confirm the OAuth redirect URI matches the CT Pros domain

tasks.title: Deploy CT Pros to its own host/domain
tasks.notes: Deploy infra exists and proven (deploy/*.sh, docker-compose.yml, docs/deployment.md) but still pointed at the cloned app. Re-target to a fresh DO droplet with CT Pros's real domain and a fresh DB.

prep.what: Spin up a fresh DigitalOcean droplet for CT Pros with its real domain and run the proven deploy scripts — the infra is ready, this is pure execution.
prep.understand: The same deploy scripts that run HighTech work here; only target IP, domain, and database differ. The t24 company info commit MUST be included before running this deploy, or production ships HighTech branding. CT Pros gets its own isolated droplet and DB, completely separate from HighTech's environment. DNS propagation can take up to 30 min.
approach: Provision a new DO droplet for CT Pros (Ubuntu 22.04, match HighTech's tier) — point CT Pros's domain DNS A record to the new IP
SSH in, clone the ct-pros-sys repo (confirm the t24 commit is included), run `deploy/*.sh` to install PHP, nginx, MySQL
Run `php artisan migrate --force && php artisan db:seed` to initialize a fresh CT Pros database
Open CT Pros's domain → confirm TLS is active, login page shows CT Pros branding, and the app is fully operational
resources: DigitalOcean dashboard — create new droplet (~$24/mo basic tier)
CT Pros domain DNS registrar — point A record to new droplet IP
`deploy/*.sh` + `docker-compose.yml` in the ct-pros-sys repo
`docs/deployment.md` — the proven step-by-step deploy guide
